coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots as new hole transport material for perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Paulo, Sofia
Stoica, Georgiana
Cambarau, Werther
Martinez-Ferrero, Eugenia
Palomares, Emilio
coredata.dc:description: The use of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in photovoltaic devices has not yet been fully explored. Herein, we report the use of CQDs in MAPI (methyl ammonium lead iodide) perovskite solar cells as a first attempt to implement this material in photovoltaic applications beyond its use as transparent conductive electrode.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Perovskite
Hole transport materials
Photovoltaic devices
Hybrid solar cells

E.M-F. thanks the Spanish MINECO for the Ramon y Cajal fellowship RYC-2010-06787 and funding through the project MAT2012-31570. Eurecat financial aid is kindly acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of CQDaq synthesis. Picture of the CQDaq under 360nm UV lamp.
Absorbance (a) and emission (b) spectra of CQDaq (solid line) and CQDiso (dashed line), respectively. Inset photo in Fig. 2b represents the CQDiso under 360nm UV lamp.
PL lifetime decay of CQDs in water (solid line) and isopropanol (dashed line). λexc =405nm, λems =452 and 514nm respectively. Solid lines represent the biexponential fit of the decays.
UV–vis and emission spectra of CQDiso (a) and cyclic voltammetry (b) of CQDs in DMF.
Depiction of the device (left) and energy levels of the materials employed (right) with the device architecture FTO/d-TiO2/mp-TiO2/MAPI/HTM/Au, where HTM is either CQDs or spiro-OMeTAD.
Current density-voltage characteristics of the MAPI devices with and without the HTMs studied in this work. The measurements have been performed in reverse scan (from higher to lower voltages).
Top view ESEM images of CQDs and spiro-OMeTAD (reference) devices.
Time constants τ1 and τ2, components B1 and B2, and average lifetime τ of CQDaq and CQDiso, respectively.
Device performance of the MAPI solar cells shown in Fig. 6.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots as new hole transport material for perovskite solar cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemical Research of Catalonia (ICIQ)
The Barcelona Institute of Science and Technology (BIST)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in photovoltaic devices has not yet been fully explored. Herein, we report the use of CQDs in MAPI (methyl ammonium lead iodide) perovskite solar cells as a first attempt to implement this material in photovoltaic applications beyond its use as transparent conductive electrode.

ce:section.ce:para: During the last decade in the areas of organic electronics and biotechnology, two new carbon based materials, namely carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have grabbed the attention of numerous research groups thanks to their potential to be used either in optoelectronic devices or bio-imaging, respectively [1–3].

In biotechnology, CQDs have revealed interesting properties like water solubility, low toxicity (especially compared with their inorganic counterpart QDs like PbS or CdSe), stability against photobleaching and better biocompatibility [4].

For optoelectronic applications, particularly since 2010, when the Nobel Prize in physics was awarded to A. Geim and K. Novoselov for their work on the 2D material graphene, many researchers have proposed their use in solar energy application such as hybrid and organic solar cells, light emitting diodes or photodetectors [3].

In this work, we study the possibility to employ a solution processed layer of CQDs as HTM in perovskite solar cells. This type of solar cell is nowadays trending topic in the photovoltaic research world mostly due to the outstanding power conversion efficiencies (15–20%) reached in just a few years from its appearance [5].

In these years, a lot of effort has been put to improve the stability and performance of the devices either by developing alternative ways to form the perovskite layer (absorber) [6–10] or employing new materials to facilitate charge transport through the device (selective contacts) [11–14]. The latter pathway is usually focused on testing and development of new HTMs, like polymers or small molecules, to be deposited via solution processes on top of the perovskite layer, being the titanium dioxide (TiO2) the most employed material as an electron transport material (ETM). This research activity could be conceived as an open race to find competitors for spiro-OMeTAD (which is right now the most used HTM in this type of cells) that can be synthesized in an easier way (fewer synthetic steps), in large scale, that do not need dopants or oxidants and, above all, that could lead to an increase in stability and performance of the solar cell.

In this paper, we propose the use of solution processed CQDs as HTM in MAPI solar cells. In Section 2 we will describe the synthesis (2.1) and the device fabrication (2.2) and in Section 3 we will show the results obtained in this study.

The synthesis was carried out via a polymerization–carbonization hydrothermal approach [15]. 1.0507g of citric acid and 0.541g of p-phenylenediamine were well dissolved in 10mL of deionized water. The solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (120mL) and heated at 200°C for 5h, with a heating ramp of 5°C/min. After the reaction, a brown-black solution was obtained. Then, the solution was centrifuged thrice at 4500rpm and the CQDs were dispersed in deionized water (CQDaq).

Fluorine doped Tin Oxide (FTO) coated glasses (resistance 8Ω/cm2) were etched using Zn powder (Alfa Aesar 98%) and a 2M solution of HCl according to the desired pattern. Afterwards, the substrates were cleaned for 15min in an ultrasound bath with deionized water with Hellmax soap, with deionized water and finally with ethanol. The substrates were dried and an UV/Ozone treatment was performed for 20min.

0.65mL of Ti (IV) isopropoxide (Sigma–Aldrich 97%) and 0.38mL of acetylacetone (Sigma–Aldrich) were mixed in 5mL of ethanol. This solution was deposited by spin coating at 3000rpm for 60s on top of the FTO. Substrates were calcined at 500°C for 30min to obtain the TiO2 dense layer (d-TiO2) having a thickness of around 50nm. Afterwards, titanium oxide-coated substrates were immersed in a 40mM TiCl4 solution at 70°C for 30min. Then, the substrates were cleaned with water and ethanol and heated at 390°C for 20 min.
A mixture of TiO2 paste (18 NR-T Dyesol) and ethanol 2:7 (w/w) was spun at 5000rpm for 30s. The substrates were heated at 500°C for 30min to form a mesoporous layer (mp-TiO2) of around 350nm.

The methylammonium iodide was synthesized as described previously [16]. The MAPI solution consists of a mixture of 3:1 (molar ratio) of methylammonium iodide and PbCl2 (Sigma–Aldrich 98%) in DMF [10]. 80μL of this solution were spun above the mp-TiO2 layer at 2000rpm for 60s. The as deposited substrates were heated at 100°C for 1h.
In order to avoid perovskite dissolution, the water dispersed CQDs were redissolved in isopropanol (CQDiso) to obtain a 2.5mg/mL solution. 80μL of CQDiso were spun at 1000rpm for 90s.

72.3mg of spiro-OMeTAD (1-Material) were dissolved in 1mL chlorobenzene with the addition of 28.8μL tert-butylpyridin and 17.5μL of lithium bis-trifluoromethanesulfonimide solution in acetonitrile (520mg/mL). The resulting solution was spun (80μL) onto the perovskite at 2000rpm for 60s.
Finally, a layer of 80nm of gold was deposited as the anode by thermal evaporation at a pressure not higher than 1×10−6 mbar.
All the steps for MAPI and HTM deposition were carried out inside a nitrogen filled glove box to avoid the excess of humidity ([O2]<100ppm and [H2O]<0.1ppm).

UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded, using a 1cm path length quartz cell in a Shimadzu UV spectrophotometer 1700 and a Fluorolog Horiba Jobin Ivon spectrophotometer, respectively. Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) measurements were carried out on an Edinburgh Instruments LifeSpec-II spectrometer equipped with the appropriated PMT detector, double subtractive monochromator and a set of picosecond pulsed diode lasers sources. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments were performed with an Autolab PGSTAT 30 electrochemical analyzer at room temperature with a three-electrode configuration in a dimethylformamide solution containing the sample (typically about 1mM) and the supporting electrolyte. A platinum (ø 1mm) disc served as the working electrode, a platinum wire (ø 1mm) and a commercial Ag/AgCl electrode being the counter and the reference electrodes respectively. 0.1M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (NBu4PF6) was employed as the supporting electrolyte. The solutions were stirred and deaerated by bubbling argon for a few minutes prior to each voltammetric measurement. The scan rate was 0.01V/s.

The J–V characteristics of the devices were measured using a Sun 2000 Solar Simulator (150W, ABET Technologies). The illumination intensity was measured to be 100mW/cm2 (equivalent to 1 Sun) with a calibrated silicon photodiode (NREL). The applied potential and cell current were measured with a Keithley 2400 digital source meter. Environmental scanning electron microscopy (ESEM) and Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDX) were carried out at low voltage (20keV) in a FEI Quanta 600 FEG microscope equipped with an EDX detector from Oxford Instruments. ImageJ software was used for surface coverage quantitative image analysis.

CQDs were synthesized following a one-step hydrothermal treatment of citric acid, as carbon source, and p-phenylenediamine as the surface passivation agent, as depicted in Fig. 1 . According to the synthesis mechanism [17], the CQDs are formed through four consecutive stages: dehydration and polymerization (both facilitated by the carboxyl groups of the citric acid), carbonization, and passivation, respectively. Initially, the molecules assemble as a result of hydrogen bonding. Then, during heating (dehydration), polymerization occurs, leading to a short single burst of nucleation. The resulting nuclei then grow by the diffusion of solutes toward the particles surfaces.

The amine molecules play a dual role as n-doping precursors and as passivating agent, which finally is translated into improved photoluminescence. Heteroatom doping (N, F, S, P, Gd) is a commonly used approach to fine-tune or obtain new photoluminescence (PL) and other physicochemical properties of photoluminescent materials [18]. Furthermore, co-doping strategies by combining Mg/N, P/N, and S/N were found to dramatically improve the photoluminescent emission, in addition to luminescence stability, high solubility, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [18]. The UV–vis spectrum of the CQDaq (solid line in Fig. 2 a) shows an absorption peak around 380 nm, very well defined for the CQDiso nanoparticles (dashed line in Fig. 2a). As reported previously [19], the peak located at 380nm is ascribed to the presence of the CO bonds and their n–π* transition, as a consequence of amides formed between NH2 and COOH, while the broad tail extending over the visible part of the spectrum likely originates from other functionalized surface groups of the CQDs.

Next, the corresponding PL spectra of both samples were recorded upon excitation at 380nm. CQDaq displayed an emission centered at 515nm (solid line in Fig. 2b). Additionally, a greenish color PL emission was observed upon excitation of the CQDaq at 360nm with an external UV lamp, as displayed in Fig. 1. On the other hand, the PL emission of CQDiso experienced a blue shift of 60nm with a maximum at 455nm displaying a bright blue color when excited with the UV lamp (360nm), as indicated by the inset photo in Fig. 2b. These results indicate that solvents with different relative polarities and dielectric constants have different influence on the optical properties of the CQDs despite both of the solvents being polar protic. In other words, a less polar solvent like the isopropanol (dielectric constant of 18 and relative polarity of 0.546, respectively [20]) will induce a blue shift of the CQDs emission with respect to water (dielectric constant of 80 and relative polarity of 1, respectively). This solvent-dependent PL emissions behavior is compatible with the previously reported study by Zhu et al. [21] when investigating GQDs. The authors registered a PL red shift from 515nm to 575nm when dissolving the carbon nanoparticles in solvents with different polarities (THF, acetone, DMF, water), as a consequence of the solvent attachment or different emissive traps on the surface of quantum dots.

Fig. 3 represents the luminescence decay profile of both the aqueous and isopropanol solutions of CQDs, respectively. This analysis provides average radiative lifetimes those contain contributions from both excitonic and trap states.

The decay curves can be fitted to a biexponential model described in Eq. (1).
(1) F(t)=B+B1exp(−t/τ1)+B2exp(−t/τ2)
where τ1 and τ2 represent the time constants and B1 and B2 represent the amplitudes of the components, respectively. The average lifetime was calculated by the expression in Eq. (2).
(2) 4τ=(B1τ1 2 +B2τ2 2)/(B1τ1 +B2τ2)
The fitting parameters B1, τ1, B2, τ2 and τ are summarized in Table 1 .

The average life time was calculated to be 3.61ns for the CQDs aqueous solution and 12.38ns for the CQDs in isopropanol pointing to the influence of the solvent on the lifetime, in line with the PL fluorescence behavior. This effect is more pronounced on the slow component, while the fast component, attributed to the radiative recombination of the excitons responsible for the fluorescence, hardly changed. Similar results have been reported for mercaptopropionic acid-capped CdTe quantum dots which displayed a significantly longer lifetime in methanol than in water [22]. These results drive us to the conclusion that the solvents affect not only the PL emission but also the lifetime of the QD nanoparticles, with emphasis on the polarity and the interaction between the functional groups on the nanoparticle surface and the solvent, respectively. The electrochemical cyclic voltammetry was performed for determining the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels of the CQDs. Fig. 4 b shows the cyclic voltammogram of CQDs solution in DMF.

The CQDs oxidation potential was determined as+0.78eV. The HOMO of the CQDs was thus calculated using the Eq. (3).
(3) EHOMO =−(4.88−E1/2,Fc,Fc+ +EOX)
where EOX is the oxidation potential of the CQDs and E1/2,Fc,Fc+ is the half-wave potential of Fc/Fc+ estimated in 0.54eV. Thus, the HOMO has a value of −5.12eV. The LUMO (−2.07eV) was then calculated by the equation 4.
(4) 5 ELUMO =EHOMO +E0–0
where E0–0 represent the 0–0 energy, which is defined as the lowest energy transition and that we can estimate from UV–vis and fluorescence emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 4a, we obtained a value of 3.05eV for E0–0 corresponding to 407nm. The HOMO energy value is above the MAPI perovskite valence band (VB) energy (-5.5eV), which ensures efficient hole transfer from the perovskite to CQDs (Fig. 6). For comparison purposes, in Fig. 5
, the HOMO LUMO levels of spiro-OMeTAD are also shown.

Photovoltaic devices were fabricated to evaluate the performance of the CQDs as HTM in comparison with spiro-OMeTAD. The J–V characteristics shown in Fig. 6, demonstrate the evidence that the CQDs layer introduces a benefit with respect to the absence of an HTM in the device, although the reference cell with spiro-OMeTAD performs much better in terms of short circuit current density (JSC), open-circuit voltage (VOC) and, thus, in power conversion efficiency (PCE). The increase in VOC and FF upon insertion of a CQD layer suggests that the latter fairly improves the contact between the perovskite and the gold electrode.
Numeric values for reverse voltage scan (see SI for both forward and reverse) are summarized in Table 2

Fig. 7 shows representative top view ESEM images of a device fabricated with CQDs, together with the spiro-OMeTAD reference solar cell. In both cases, by simple optical evaluation of the images, it can be observed that their surface was not fully covered by the perovskite layer. Indeed, an image treatment with the ImageJ software revealed poor perovskite coverage over the mp-TiO2 surface, i.e. around 60% for the spiro-OMeTAD cell and 50% for the CQDs device, respectively (Figs. SI1a, SI1b). The relative poor coverage of the mp-TiO2 could be responsible for the low efficiencies of the devices compared to ones reported in the literature as indicated previously by other groups [9]. The fact that the JSC of the device does not increase with the introduction of a CQD layer could be due to different factors. Even if the SEM images do not give us a proof of evidence, we consider that the CQD layer is not homogeneous since single dots tend to aggregate forming island-like domains. This configuration could lead to a bad charge transfer between the perovskite and the top contact. Another possible reason for the low photocurrent could be attributed to the type of ligand employed for the CQD (p-phenylenediamine) which could be responsible of a lack of charge mobility.

The spectrum profile of the elemental mapping by EDX clearly shows the presence of Pb and I, while Cl was not detected in either of the devices (Fig. SI2). The quantitative analysis revealed a molar ratio of Pb/I=3.45 for the CQDs and Pb/I=3.7 for the spiro-OMeTAD, respectively, which is slightly higher than the theoretical ratio of the MAPI solar cells (Pb/I=3). These results demonstrate that the perovskite was successfully formed during the synthesis step.

In conclusion, the synthesis and optical and electrochemical characterization of CQDs have been extensively described. The cyclic voltammetry measurements revealed that the HOMO and LUMO energy values of the CQDs are appropriate to ensure both efficient hole transfer and electron blocking capability from the perovskite to CQDs. Preliminary photovoltaic devices based on CQDs as HTM have been fabricated for the first time and a power conversion efficiency as high as 3% has been achieved. A known limitation like poor perovskite coverage over the mp-TiO2 surface, revealed by ESEM analysis of the devices, could be a possible reason for the relatively low performance of the solar cell. Taking this fact into account, we believe that further optimizations in the device fabrication together with a finer tuning of the CQDs properties could promote a brand new type of HTM with very high potential for perovskite solar cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank ICIQ, Generalitat Catalunya, and Spanish MINECO through Severo Ochoa Excellence Accreditation 2014–2018 (SEV-2013-0319) and project CTQ2013-47183R for the financial support. E.M-F. thanks the Spanish MINECO for the Ramon y Cajal fellowship RYC-2010-06787 and funding through the project MAT2012-31570. Eurecat financial aid is kindly acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.synthmet.2016.04.025.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
